1: Psalm 40 is structurally unusual: it combines a completed thanksgiving (Psalms 40:1-10) for a past deliverance with a new lament petition (Psalms 40:11-17), suggesting either that the same person experiences a new crisis after being delivered, or (more likely) that the psalm is designed for repeated liturgical use by anyone who has been delivered and now faces a new crisis. The superscription "To the director of music, A Psalm of David" places it in the royal liturgical collection. "I waited patiently (qawwōh qiwwîṯî)" — the cognate infinitive absolute for emphasis: "waiting, I waited," i.e., I waited with determined perseverance. Qāwāh (to wait with hope, tension like a rope stretched taut) is the patience-virtue of Ps 37 applied here as biographical testimony: "I did what Ps 37 commanded, and YHWH responded."
2: "He lifted me out of the pit of destruction (bôr šāʾôn) from the muddy bog (tît hayyāwēn); he set my feet on a rock (seleʿ) and made my steps firm" — bôr šāʾôn (pit of noise/destruction) — the underworld imagery of Sheol, the pit of death (also Psalms 88:3-6; Psalms 143:3; Lamentations 3:53-55; Isaiah 38:17-18). Tît hayyāwēn (miry clay, muddy bog) — the sinking quicksand-like mud of the riverbed; the same imagery as Psalms 69:2, 14 ("I sink in the deep mire"). The rescue from the pit is simultaneously personal (from illness, danger, or despair) and eschatological (from the realm of death itself). "Set my feet on a rock (seleʿ)" — the contrast between the bog and the rock is total: the unstable, sinking surface replaced by the immovable foundation. Seleʿ (rock, cliff) is also used as a divine title (Psalms 18:2; Psalms 19:14; Psalms 31:2-3).
3: "He put a new song (šîr ḥādāš) in my mouth, praise to our God; many will see and be filled with awe (yîrāʾû) and will put their trust in the LORD" — the šîr ḥādāš (new song) is a recurring motif in the Psalter (Psalms 33:3; Psalms 40:3; Psalms 96:1; Psalms 98:1; Psalms 144:9; Psalms 149:1; Isaiah 42:10; Revelation 5:9; Psalms 14:3) for the song of fresh divine deliverance. Each new act of rescue requires a new song because the old songs do not capture the particular character of the new event. "Many will see and be filled with awe" — the public dimension of private deliverance: the testimony of the rescued one becomes the occasion for others's faith (cf. Psalms 22:27-28, 31). The public thanksgiving psalm is a community event: the rescued person's testimony functions as evangelion — good news that produces trust in others.
4: "Blessed is the one who makes the LORD his trust (mibṭaḥô) and does not turn to the proud (rĕhābîm) or to those who follow falsehood (śāṭê kāzāb)" — the beatitude form connecting trust-in-YHWH with avoiding two alternative orientations. Rĕhābîm (the proud, the arrogant) — from rāhab (to act stormily, to be arrogant, to storm); in Psalms 89:10 Rĕhab is the mythological sea-monster crushed by YHWH (also Isaiah 51:9). To trust in the proud is to trust in the chaos-forces that YHWH defeats. Śāṭê kāzāb (those who turn aside to falsehood) — idolaters who turn to šeqer (falsehood) are turning to non-gods, the same contrast Psalms 31:6 makes: "those who cling to empty vanities abandon the steadfast love that belongs to them."
5: "You have multiplied your wonderful deeds (niplĕʾôtêkā) and your thoughts (maḥšĕbôtêkā) toward us, O LORD my God; no one can compare to you. If I were to declare and speak them, they are too many to count" — the hymnic response to overwhelming divine grace. Niplāʾôt (wonderful works, miracles, marvels) — the category of acts so beyond human power and expectation that they require a new vocabulary; used for the Exodus signs (Exodus 3:20; Psalms 34:10; Psalms 78:12), creation (Psalms 136:4), and ongoing providence. "Your thoughts toward us (maḥšĕbôtêkā ʾēlênû)" — divine intentionality, the active mental engagement of God with his people; Jeremiah 29:11: "I know the plans (maḥšābôt) I have for you, plans for shalom and not for harm." The incomparability formula (mî kāmôkā, "none compares to you") appears in Exodus 15:11; Psalms 35:10; Isaiah 40:18; Psalms 46:5; Micah 7:18.
6: "Sacrifice (zebaḥ) and grain offering (minḥāh) you did not want; but you have given me opened ears (ʾoznayim kārîṯā lî); burnt offering (ʿôlāh) and sin offering (ḥaṭṭāʾāh) you have not asked for" — the most theologically freighted verse of the psalm. Four cultic categories are listed and negated: the zebaḥ (peace offering/slaughter), the minḥāh (grain offering), the ʿôlāh (whole burnt offering), and the ḥaṭṭāʾāh (sin offering) — together they cover the major sacrifice types. The negation is not an anti-temple polemic (as Amos 5:21-25 might be read) but a relativization: what God truly desires is not these per se but what they are meant to express — the obedient heart. "Opened ears" (ʾoznayim kārîṯā, literally "ears you have dug/bored for me") — possibly an allusion to the ear-piercing of the lifelong servant (Exodus 21:6; Deuteronomy 15:17), or simply "you have made me receptive to your instruction." The Septuagint (LXX) renders this as "a body you have prepared for me" — a significant divergence that Hebrews 10:5-7 applies to Christ.
7: "Then I said, 'Here I am; in the scroll of the book (bĕmĕgillat-sēper) it is written about me'" — the "here I am" (hinnēh-bāʾṯî, I have come) is the classic response of readiness and availability (Genesis 22:1, 7, 11 — Abraham's response to the divine summons; Isaiah 6:8 — Isaiah's response to the divine call). "The scroll of the book" — likely the Torah scroll (cf. Deuteronomy 17:18-20, the king's copy of the law); the psalmist presents his existence as already inscribed in the divine purposes contained in the Torah. Hebrews 10:7 quotes this verse (following the LXX) as Christ's words at his incarnation: "Then I said, 'Behold, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written of me in the scroll of the book.'" The christological application reads the "here I am" as Christ's entry into the world in perfect obedience to the will expressed in Scripture.
8: "I delight (ḥāpāṣtî) to do your will, O my God; your instruction (tôrātĕkā) is within my heart (bĕṯôk mēʿāy)" — the positive counterpart of Psalms 40:6's negations: not external sacrifice but inward delight in obedience. Ḥāpēṣ (to delight in, to desire, to take pleasure in) — the word used for YHWH's "not delighting in" sacrifice in Psalms 40:6 is here used for the speaker's delight in YHWH's will — a mirroring. "Your instruction (tôrāh) within my heart (bĕtôk mēʿāy)" — the internalization of Torah promised in Jeremiah 31:33 ("I will put my law within them") and described as the goal of Psalms 37:31. The word mēʿîm (innermost parts, bowels, womb) indicates the deepest level of the person — not intellectual assent but the depths of the being transformed by Torah. Hebrews 10:7 applies this to Christ: the incarnate Son's delight in doing the Father's will is the fulfillment of the psalmist's aspiration.
9: "I have announced (bissartî) righteousness in the great assembly (bĕqāhāl rāb); I have not held my lips back — LORD, you know it" — bāsar (to bring good news, to announce, to herald) — the verbal root of bĕśôrāh (good news, gospel) and the Septuagint's euangelizomai (to evangelize, to proclaim the gospel). The righteous proclamation (ṣedeq) announced in the great assembly is the public declaration of deliverance — the thanksgiving psalm as public gospel. The "great assembly" is the congregation gathered at the temple, the same context as the todah (thanksgiving offering). "LORD, you know it" — the divine witness to the psalmist's public obedience; the same appeal to divine knowledge as Psalms 139:1-4.
10: "I have not hidden your righteousness (ṣidqāṯĕkā) in my heart; I have declared your faithfulness (ʾĕmûnāṯĕkā) and your salvation (tĕšûʿāṯĕkā); I have not concealed your steadfast love (ḥasdĕkā) or your truth (wĕʾamiṯĕkā) from the great assembly" — five divine attributes named and proclaimed: righteousness, faithfulness, salvation, steadfast love, and truth. These five together constitute the core vocabulary of YHWH's covenant character (cf. Psalms 36:5-6 where four of these appear). The five-fold "I have not hidden... I have not concealed" structure is a testimony of complete transparency — the psalmist has not withheld any dimension of the divine character from the public proclamation. This is the anti-concealment, the opposite of the "hiding" that characterizes the wicked (Psalms 36:4; Psalms 52:2).
11: "As for you, LORD, do not withhold your compassion (raḥămêkā) from me; let your steadfast love and truth continually protect me" — the transition from thanksgiving (Psalms 40:1-10) to petition (Psalms 40:11-17) is marked by "As for you" (ʾattāh) — a pivot from the psalmist's proclamation of what YHWH has done to a new petition for what YHWH should do. "Your compassion (raḥămêkā)" — raḥam (womb-compassion, tender mercy) is YHWH's maternal emotion, the feeling of a parent for the child borne in the womb (Isaiah 49:15: "Can a mother forget the baby at her breast and have no compassion on the child she has borne?"). The accumulated attributes — compassion, ḥesed, truth — form a request that the same divine character that was proclaimed (Psalms 40:9-10) now be directed personally toward the speaker in the new crisis.
12: "For countless evils (rāʿôt ʾên-mispar) have closed in around me (ʾăpāpûnî); my iniquities (ʿăwōnōṯay) have caught up with me (hissîgûnî) until I cannot see clearly (ʿad-lōʾ-yākolṯî lirʾôṯ)" — the new crisis is described with the same surrounding-and-overwhelming imagery as Ps 38's illness (Psalms 38:4, 8). The admission that "my iniquities have caught up with me" is one of the Psalter's honest acknowledgments that the speaker's own sins contribute to the crisis. "Until I cannot see clearly" — the inability to see is both physical (despair darkens the perception) and moral (sin distorts the vision). "They are more than the hairs of my head (rabbû miśśaʿărôt rōʾšî)" — the proverbial uncountability of hair (Genesis 16:10; Luke 12:7; Acts 27:34) applied to sins and troubles; the human cannot number them but God can.
13: "Please, LORD, come to rescue me; LORD, hurry to help me (ḥûšāh lĕʿezrāṯî)" — verses 13-17 of Psalm 40 are virtually identical to Psalm 70 (with minor textual variations), which circulates independently as a shorter emergency petition. The appearance of a complete independent psalm within Psalm 40 raises questions about composition: either Ps 40 incorporates an older petition (Ps 70), or Ps 70 is an excerpt from Ps 40, or both draw on a common liturgical prayer. The "hurry to help me" (ḥûšāh lĕʿezrāṯî) prayer appears in Psalms 22:19; Psalms 38:22; Psalms 71:12 — an emergency formula for urgent divine assistance. The liturgical portability of the petition (usable independently as Ps 70) suggests it was a standard emergency prayer in the temple liturgy.
14: "Let those who seek to take my life be put to shame and confounded (yēbōšû wĕyaḥpĕrû); let those who wish me harm be turned back in disgrace (yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikālamû)" — the double shame-pair: bôš (shame, confusion) and ḥāpar (to be ashamed, to be embarrassed) in the first pair; sûg ʾāḥôr (turn backward, retreat) and kālam (be humiliated, disgraced) in the second. All four terms describe the public reversal of honor — the enemy who confidently sought the psalmist's life being publicly exposed, turned back, and shamed in the community's sight. The honor-shame reversal is the social dimension of divine vindication: not merely the psalmist's private survival but the public demonstration of the enemy's miscalculation.
15: "Let those who mock me with 'Aha! Aha!' (heʾāḥ heʾāḥ) be horrified by their own shame" — the gloating exclamation heʾāḥ appears in Psalms 35:21, 25 and Ezekiel 25:3; Psalms 26:2; Psalms 36:2 — always the triumphant cry of one who gloats over another's ruin. The prayer that those who gloat will be "horrified (yāšōmmû) by their own shame" is the shame-reversal petition: the emotion they intended to produce in the psalmist (horror, shock) lands on themselves instead. Šāmam (to be desolate, to be devastated, to be appalled) is one of the strongest shock-responses in Hebrew — the same verb used for the desolation of the temple after the destruction (Lamentations 1:4, 13, 16) and for the Servant's appearance that "appalled" many (Isaiah 52:14).
16: "Let all who seek you (mĕbaqqĕšêkā) rejoice and be glad in you; let those who love your salvation (tĕšûʿāṯĕkā) always say, 'Great is the LORD!'" — the positive counterpart to Psalms 40:14-15's shame-petitions. While the wicked are shamed, "all who seek you" (the covenant community of dōrĕšê YHWH) will rejoice. The seeking (bāqaš) of God is the positive covenant orientation — actively pursuing the divine presence (Amos 5:4-6; Isaiah 55:6-7; Psalms 27:4, 8). "Those who love your salvation" — the community's love for tĕšûʿāh (salvation) is the communal counterpart of the psalmist's personal experience of rescue (Psalms 40:1-3). "Great is the LORD!" — the proclamation that emerges from witnessed deliverance; the same shout that accompanies the ark procession (2 Samuel 6:2) and the divine enthronement psalms (Psalms 47:2; 95-100).
17: "As for me, I am poor (ʿānî) and needy (ʾebyôn); yet the Lord thinks of me (yaḥšōb). You are my help and my deliverer — do not be slow (al-ʾaḥar), my God" — the final verse holds together the psalmist's poverty and the Lord's intentional attention. ʿĀnî wĕʾebyôn (poor and needy) — the standard paired designation of the socially and economically vulnerable (Psalms 37:14 above; Psalms 35:10; Psalms 70:5; Psalms 86:1; Psalms 109:22; Isaiah 41:17; Amos 4:1; Psalms 8:4, 6). "Yet the Lord thinks of me (yaḥšōb lî ʾădōnāy)" — the divine thought (ḥāšab) directed toward the poor person is the theodicy resolution compressed into one line: the great God who has "multiplied wonderful deeds and thoughts toward us" (Psalms 40:5) also specifically thinks of the individual poor person. Ps 70 ends with the same verse, confirming it as the standard emergency-close of this petition type. "Do not be slow (al-ʾaḥar)" — the urgent "do not delay" that brings the psalm to its final note of urgent, hopeful prayer.
